city.title: Den Haag
city.description:
The Hague is home to the Dutch parliament, the International Court of Justice, and a wartime resistance history that speaks directly to the stakes of democracy. The Hague has always been a city where power is expected to explain itself.
  
It is the seat of a parliament that has been arguing in public since the 13th century. It is a city of courts, ministries, embassies, protest fields, archives, cafés, churches, bookshops, prisons, memorial stones, and open squares. It is a city where law is not treated as an abstract idea, but as something people build, test, violate, defend, revise, and demand again.
This is a city of exiles, dissidents, jurists, organizers, diplomats, prisoners, protesters, refugees, voters, and witnesses. It is a city where ideas about freedom of thought, public accountability, international law, peace, and resistance have been written, argued, banned, defended, and rewritten.
  
It is the city that built the Peace Palace on the belief that law could be made to apply to states as well as citizens. It is the city where women gathered in 1915, in the middle of a world war, to insist that peace and democracy could not be negotiated without them. It is the city where people still gather at Malieveld when power needs to hear them.
It is also a city marked by occupation.
  
When the Nazis occupied The Hague in 1940, they took over the Binnenhof and raised their flag over the political heart of the Netherlands. They turned part of the Scheveningen prison into the Oranjehotel, where resistance members, political prisoners, Jewish people, communists, Jehovah’s Witnesses, and others targeted by the regime were interrogated, imprisoned, and sent onward. They used records, files, identity systems, and bureaucracy to make persecution efficient. They deported more than 12,000 Jewish residents of this city. Around 1,700 of them were children.
But The Hague is not only a city of what was destroyed. It is a city of what people kept trying to protect.
  
Resistance here looked like forged papers, illegal newspapers, hidden addresses, disrupted records, quiet refusals, public protest, legal argument, care networks, and names remembered in stone. It looked like women crossing borders to organize for peace. It looked like Jewish residents sustaining community under persecution. It looked like people from the Dutch Caribbean, Indonesia, Suriname, and other parts of the Dutch colonial world insisting that freedom and law could not be reserved only for Europe. It looked like families, teachers, neighbors, organizers, and ordinary people making choices under impossible pressure.
It also looks like what we do now.

Today, threats to democracy do not always arrive wearing uniforms. They arrive through book bans, voter suppression, attacks on courts, dehumanizing language, intimidation, detention without accountability, rewritten histories, corrupted records, and the slow normalization of lies. They arrive when people are told that some stories should not be read, some voters should not count, some communities should not be heard, and some forms of power should not have to answer.

YES.WE.VOTE Den Haag moves through that argument.

We begin in a bookstore, because democracy requires the right to read. We request our ballots, because citizenship does not end at the border. We search for banned books, remove lies from speeches, read names in public, draft and revise a manifesto, gather at a protest field, question records, witness opened doors, and return to the place where power is supposed to answer.
This is not just a route through The Hague.
It is a route through the question every democracy has to keep asking:

Who gets to read?
Who gets to be counted?
Who gets to be heard?
Who gets to be remembered?
And who is power allowed to hide?
We walk through that question today.
We vote to answer it.
